summary:
The D'mt Kingdom (c. 900–400 BC) was an ancient kingdom in the northern Ethiopian Highlands and Eritrea, known from inscriptions and archaeological remains that show it was one of the earliest complex societies in sub-Saharan Africa. D'mt appears to have been strongly influenced by the South Arabian [[event:1974|Sabaean Kingdom Yemen]] Sabaean kingdom across the Red Sea — its inscriptions use South Arabian script and language variants, its religious practices show similarities to Sabaean polytheism, and its material culture includes South Arabian-style temples and stone platforms.

The kingdom's agricultural base was supported by advanced irrigation technology, including extensive pre-Aksumite stone terracing of the highland slopes, evidence of ox-drawn plows (one of the earliest agricultural innovations in sub-Saharan Africa), and cultivation of teff, emmer wheat, and barley. D'mt represents the proto-historic phase of what would eventually develop into the powerful Aksumite kingdom (see [[event:1160|Aksumite Proto Kingdom]]). The precise nature of the Sabaean-D'mt relationship is debated: some scholars see it as actual colonization or migration from South Arabia, others as trade contact and elite imitation of prestige culture. The kingdom is poorly documented compared to contemporary civilizations and remains an active area of archaeological research.
